reasoning: The question requires Friedrich Merz to "cease" to be Chancellor, which implies he must first take on the position. Then, he must lose it before December 31, 2025. Given the collapse of the German coalition on November 6, 2024, elections will be held in early 2025, making him the overwhelming favorite to assume the chancellorship by mid-2025. The probability of him resigning, dying, or being removed within his first 6 to 9 months in office is extraordinarily low. No post-war German Chancellor has ever left office in their first year. Merz is 69, in seemingly good health, and has sought this role for over two decades, making voluntary resignation highly unlikely. A constructive vote of no confidence is historically rare (happened once in 1982) and requires a solid opposition majority, which would not naturally coalesce soon after a new government is formed. Mortality/health crisis risk for a 69-year-old gives a baseline of around 1.5-2%, and adding tail risks of extreme scandals brings the true probability to roughly 3-4%. The Polymarket price of 6% likely contains a small risk premium typical for low-probability outlier events. Therefore, a 4% estimate is well-supported.
